title: Light-emitting devices based on solid electrolytes and polyelectrolytes
abstract:
Light-emitting Devices Based on Solid
Electrolytes and Polyelectrolytes
Dieter Neher,* Johannes GruÈner, Vera CimrovaÂ,† Wolfgang Schmidt,
Rudy Rulkens‡ and Ulrich Lauter§
Max-Planck-Institut fuÈr Polymerforschung, Ackermannweg 10, D-55128 Mainz, Germany
ABSTRACT

The properties of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) based on
organic layers containing mobile ions, so-called light-
emitting electrochemical cells (LECs), are reviewed.
These
devices have
some
unique
properties:
their
current±voltage characteristics are antisymmetric with
respect to the origin and they emit light under both
forward and reverse bias. The physical processes involved
in the emission from LECs are discussed in terms of a
thermodynamic model. Recent work on blends of
luminescent and ion-conducting polymers is summar-
ized. In addition, the properties of novel single-
component LECs and polyelectrolyte-based devices are
presented. The results show that LECs with perfor-
mances superior to that of conventional LED devices can
be fabricated, but questions concerning the transient
behavior and degradation mechanisms persist. 1998
John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: light-emitting diodes; light-emitting electro-
chemical cell; ion-conducting polymers; solid electro-
lytes; polyelectrolyte
BACKGROUND

In 1995 Pei et al. published a novel light-emitting
device concept [1]. In the original devices a
luminescent conjugated polymer, poly(5-(2'-ethyl-
hexyloxy)-2-methoxy-1,4-phenylene
vinylene)
(MEH-PPV), was blended with ion-conducting
poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) (Scheme 1) and lithium
trifluoromethanesulfonate
(LiCF3SO3)
salt
was
added. Sandwich devices were fabricated in the
same manner as conventional LEDs: an 250nm
thick layer of the blend was spin-coated onto an
indium tin oxide (ITO) coated glass substrate and
an aluminum electrode was evaporated on top of
the polymer film. These devices, so-called polymer
light-emitting electrochemical cells (LECs), had
some unique properties:
(1) The current voltage characteristics were
antisymmetric about the origin (no recti®ca-
tion), even though electrodes with different
work-functions had been used.
(2) Light emission was observed for forward
bias (ITO wired as the anode) as well as for
reverse bias.
(3) The onset voltages for appreciable current
and for detection of visible light were equal
for both forward and reverse bias and were
CCC 1042±7147/98/070461±15 $17.50
1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 10 June 1997
Accepted 3 July 1997
POLYMERS FOR ADVANCED TECHNOLOGIES
Polym. Adv. Technol. 9, 461±475 (1998)
† Permanent address: Institute of Macromolecular Chemistry,
Academy of Sciences of the Czech Republic, Heyrovsky Sq. 2,
16206 Prague 6, Czech Republic.
‡ Present address: DSM Research, PO Box 18, 6060 MD Geleen, The
Netherlands.
§ Present address: University of Massachusetts, Amherst, MA
01003, USA.
* Correspondence to: Dieter Neher, Max-Planck-Institut fuÈr Poly-
merforschung, Ackermannweg 10, D-55128 Mainz, Germany.
Contract grant sponsor: EC.
Contract grant sponsor: Brite-Euram.
 almost identical to the band gap of the
polymer.
(4) External quantum ef®ciencies (de®ned as
the number of observed photons per injected
charge carrier) were high (approx. 2% for
MEH-PPV blended with PEO and Li salt)
and luminance levels were greater than
100cd/m2, comparable with devices of pure
MEH-PPV between ITO and Ca.
(5) The onset voltage was almost independent
of layer thickness. In fact, light emission
could be observed in a surface comb con®g-
uration with interdigitated gold electrodes
(spacing 15mm). In this case light was
emitted from a 1±3mm thick zone placed
between the gold contacts.
These unusual properties were explained by a
two-step process. When a sufficiently high voltage
is applied across the layer, the conjugated polymer
is oxidized at the cathode and reduced at the anode.
The p-type and n-type doped layers so formed are
stabilized by the counterions available in the ion-
conducting phase. Under the influence of the
applied field these regions extend towards the
center of the layer, forming an electrochemically
induced p±n junction. Here, the p-type carriers
(holes) and n-type carriers (electrons) meet and
recombine by emission of photons. Since then,
several different types of polymers and different
device concepts have been explored.
This review is devided into five parts. In the
first part, oxidation and reduction of molecules in
solution with and without the presence of counter-
ions are briefly introduced. These basic concepts
are applied to the situation of a solid polymer layer
sandwiched between two metal electrodes. The
steady-state conditions of solid electrochemical
cells are then discussed. The second part sum-
marizes properties of LECs based on blends of a
luminescent polymer and an ion-conducting poly-
mer. In the third part luminescent solid electrolytes
based on single-compound layers are introduced.
The next part discusses light-emitting devices
made from polyelectrolytes either by conventional
spin-coating or by the self-assembly technique. The
final section summarizes recent work on electro-
generated chemiluminescence in solid films.
THERMODYNAMIC TREATMENT OF
CHARGE TRANSFER REATIONS

electrodes and P leads to the formation of cations
P (oxidation) or anions Pÿ (reduction). The

of
a

thermodynamic equilibrium is established at a
certain interfacial ion concentration, which can be
deduced from thermodynamic considerations as
follows: The electrochemical potential ~ of any

where aP and aP are the activities of the neutral and
charged molecules which can be replaced by the
potentials at standard conditions (c = 1mol/l, room
temperature). Equations (3) and (4) define the
equilibrium concentrations at the metal surface:
Since me/M is the work necessary to bring or
(remove) one mole of electrons into the neutral
metal, its value is equivalent to the work-function
defined by the ionization energy of the molecule:
(vac is the vacuum level) yielding the ionization
Equation (8) also defines the energetic position
of the HOMO of the molecules in solution relative
to the vacuum level (see Fig. 1b). With these
definitions eq. (5) can be rewritten as:
The density of cations at the metal interface
depends purely on the difference between the
work-function of the metal and the ionization
energy of the molecules, irrespective of any addi-
tional potential applied to the metal. A similar
relation can be written for the reduction process:
affinity (given in units of eV) for the molecules in
solution. Under real conditions both reduction and
oxidation will occur. The combination of eqs (9)
and (11) finally leads to the important relation
This equation defines a quasi-Fermi energy 'F
of the solution. If all molecules are neutral, 'F will
be the arithmetic average of the ionization energy
and electron affinity. In other words the electronic
quasi-Fermi potential of a solution of neutral
molecules lies in the middle of the molecular
HOMO and the LUMO-positions. Increasing, for
example the concentration cP of cations, moves 'F
At equilibrium the net flow of charges across
the interface is zero. Application of an external
electric bias will disturb the equilibrium. According
FIGURE 1. Illustration of charge transfer reactions between a redox-active chemical species P in solution
and a metal surface. P can be either a molecule or a segment of a polymer chain. This species should be in
its neutral state in the initial non-radical form. Electrochemical equilibrium is established by either
oxidation (forming P) or reduction (yielding Pÿ) of P via electron transfer across the solution/metal
interface (a). The charge transfer rates kox and kred depend on the distance d the electron has to cross
during the transfer reaction. The metal is characterized by the work function 'M (b). The electronic structure
of P is simpli®ed by the positions of the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbit) and LUMO (lowest
unoccupied molecular orbit) states. Arrows indicate the removal of one electron from the HOMO
(ionization energy I) and the deposition of one additional electron into the LUMO (electron af®nity Ag).
Polym. Adv. Technol. 9, 461±475 (1998)
LEDs Based on Solid Electrolytes and Polyelectrolytes / 463
to Marcus theory the rate k of electron transfer over
the distance d is given by [2]:
Besides a pre-exponential factor describing the
quantum mechanical distance dependence of par-
ticle tunneling, the electron transfer rate is given by
the free energy barrier DG*. Marcus theory de-
scribes DG* in terms of the reorganization energy 
(not considered here) and the free enthalpy change
DG0(d) for the electron transfer process:
Df is the potential difference the electron has to
cross during the transfer process. DG0(0) (free
enthalpy difference at the surface) can be calculated
from the difference in the electrochemical poten-
tials of the chemical species before and after
electron transfer as given above. For the transfer
of, for example, an electron from a metal surface to
a neutral molecule (or polymer segment), DG0 is
equal to the difference between the electron affinity
of the molecule and the work-function of the metal.
For most luminescent molecules and polymers
used in the fabrication of LEDs Ag is approx. 2±
3eV while most air-stable metals have work-
functions between 4.3 and 5.5eV. Consequently
DG0 is larger then zero. According to eqs (13) and
(14) electron transfer can be enhanced by an
external potential Df > 0 and transfer will occur
either by thermal activation over small distances or
by ``isoenergetical'' tunneling to molecules located
at larger distances d. If the bias is applied across a
solution layer with a low concentration cP and cPÿ,
the potential will drop homogeneously across the
solution. Since electron tunneling distances are in
the sub-nm range, large external biases have to be
applied to compensate for the difference in DG0(0)
and to establish large electron transfer rates.
In the liquid electrochemical cell this problem is
solved by adding an appropriate amount of salt. As
a consequence the potential between the metal and
the solution drops across a rather small distance.
Figure 2 shows ion concentration, potential (f) and
charge () distributions in a direction perpendicu-
lar to the electrode plane for the Gouy±Chapman±
Stern model (see, e.g. [3]). The interfacial layer
consists of a double layer of immobilized ions
(Helmholtz plane) followed by a diffusive layer.
The exact treatment of electron transfer needs to
take into account the specific structure of the
interfacial region. The simplest model assumes
that the electroactive redox species are located at
electron transfer occurs. Therefore, the driving
force is the potential difference between the metal
and the OHP (fM ÿ f2). The width  of the
diffusive layer is determined by the ionic strength
where I is given in mol/l. For jZij = 1, e = 3 and
c = 1mmol/l the diffusive double layer will extend
more than 1nm into the solution and decreases to
values comparable to the thickness of the immobile
layer ( < 1nm) for c > 0.1mol/l.
This theoretical framework can be translated
directly into the processes envolved in running a
conventional polymer LED (without added salt)
and the LEC. In the solid layer the mobile charges
are holes and electrons. In the molecular picture
favored here, these charges are represented by the
radical cationic and anionic states of the polymer
segments. The motion of charged molecules in the
liquid layer then corresponds to the charge transfer
between polymer segments in the solid film. The
FIGURE 2. Schematic models of ions, potential f and
charge  across the interface between a metal and an
electrolyte for non-speci®c adsorption [3]. In the simplest
model electron transfer is driven by the potential difference
between the metal (fM) and the potential f2 at the outer
Polym. Adv. Technol. 9, 461±475 (1998)
charge transport states for holes and electrons are
defined by the position of the polymer HOMO and
LUMO. The concentrations of cations and anions,
cP and cPÿ, translate into the electron and hole
densities. Electron transfer between the metal
electrodes and the molecules in solution and charge
injection into the solid films should therefore
depend on the same physical quantities, namely
the work-function of the metal, the energy levels of
the polymer species and the specific form of the
potential drop across the interfaces.
Charge injection and electroluminescence (EL)
threshold conditions of conventional LEDs in the
limit of low electron and hole densities (rigid band
approximation) has been extensively studied by
Parker [5]. At high electric fields, charges are
injected by tunneling through a triangular barrier
(according to eqs 13 and 14) as defined by the
positions of the HOMO and LUMO of the polymer
(with respect to the work functions of the metals)
and the potential drop across the layer (Fig. 3).
Consequently, the onset voltage of an LED depends
drastically on the choice of the anode and cathode
materials, the electronic structure of the used
polymer and on film thickness.
A model for the steady-state operation of LECs
has recently been published by Smith [6]. At zero
bias, charge transfer occurs in order to adjust the
position of the quasi-Fermi level of the polymer
layer (as defined by eq. 12) to the work-function of
the electrodes. At the same time charged ionic
double layers form at the two electrodes according
to eqs (14) and (15). There exists a field-free region
in the device center. As for the liquid electro-
chemical cell described above, the width of the
double layer will depend on the concentration
of the added salt. For a bulk concentration of
dissociated salt cations and anions of approx.
more than 10nm into the polymer [6]. Increasing
the ion concentration in the bulk up to approx.
5  1017 cmÿ3 reduces the layer thickness to a few
When a small voltage is applied, the voltage
drop across the double layers increases, but the
center of the device still remains field-free (Fig. 4a).
Transport of charges in the layer will be purely
diffusive and driven by the gradient in charge
through the device. When the applied voltage
approaches a value corresponding to the polymer
bandgap, the potential drop at the surface becomes
equal to the difference between the cathode work-
function and the position of the LUMO (and equal
to the difference between the anode work-function
and the position of the HOMO) and effective
charge transfer occurs through the surface double
layer. This causes a rapid increase in the hole and
electron densities. At the same time the quasi-Fermi
level of the polymer layer close to the cathode and
anode approaches the positions of the LUMO and
HOMO level, respectively. Upon further increase
in voltage, the potential drop at the surface remains
almost constant (the region of the polymer close to
the interface is in quasi-equilibrium with the
contact) and a junction forms in the middle of the
layer (Fig. 4b). In the junction region, the concen-
tration of salt anions and cations is rather low and
the potential drop across the junction is essentially
caused by the large excess of holes and electrons on
both sides. The resulting field drives the electrons
and holes into the junction region, where they
This models predicts well the onset behavior
and the narrow emission zone as observed experi-
mentally. A severe limitation of the model by Smith
is that it assumes an ohmic contact between the two
FIGURE 3. Schematic model for the injection of charges
into a solid organic layer according to Parker [5]. Injection
occurs by Fowler±Nordheim tunneling across a triangular
potential barrier, which is de®ned by the electric ®eld across
the layer and the difference between the work-function of
the electrode material and the energetic position of the
corresponding charge transport states in the ®lm (HOMO
for holes and LUMO for electrons).
FIGURE 4. Energy level diagrams of a LEC under bias
lower than necessary for junction formation or for a bias
above the junction formation threshold (according to Smith
Polym. Adv. Technol. 9, 461±475 (1998)
LEDs Based on Solid Electrolytes and Polyelectrolytes / 465
metal electrodes and the polymer layer. In this case
an ``infinte'' reservoir of charges is present at the
interfaces and the current is limited by the charge
transport within the film. Transport-limited beha-
vior has indeed been observed, e.g. hole injection
and transport through MEH-PPV between ITO
electrodes [7]. In most cases, however, injection-
limited behavior was found to determine the device
properties. Since there is no potential drop directly
at the polymer±metal interface the equilibrium
charge concentrations at this interface are given
by eqs (9) and (11) even in the presence of mobile
ions. An injection barrier will always be present for
any DG* > 0, independent of ion concentration.
Thus, the electron transfer rate across the interface
is predicted to limit the current density for many
materials even in LECs if the width of the double
layer is larger than the typical electron tunneling
distance (for a low salt concentration).
As outlined in the introductory paragraph, Pei
et al. have explained the properties of LECs by the
formation of a p±n junction within the film. It is
important to point out that the LEC differs from the
conventional inorganic p±n LED in several areas. In
the inorganic p±n type LED, mobile charges
(electrons and holes) are already present in the
bulk prior to contact with the electrodes. These
charges are introduced by doping, that is by a
charge transfer from the dopant species to the
semiconducting material. In the LEC (particularly
for large bandgap materials), mobile charges are
almost exclusively injected from the electrodes
(electrochemical doping). The process is, in princi-
ple, equivalent to the chemical doping of polymers,
electron transfer between the conjugated polymer
backbone and an oxidant or reductant. The func-
tion of the added ions in the LEC is solely to
establish the double layer at the interfaces of the
electrodes and to provide counterions for the
electrochemical doping process. The performance
of the LEC is thus independent of the chemical
nature of the ions, as long as they do not undergo
chemical reactions. There is no charge transfer
between the added salt ions and the polymer or in
other words, the ions do not dope the polymer.
Second, the conventional p±n junction is formed by
the space charge field due to ionized dopants. In
contrast to this, the space charge field in the LEC
junction is due to an excess of holes and electrons
WWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW
Following the pioneering work of Pei et al., several
publications deal with the electrical and emission
properties of LEC devices based on polymer blends
[8±16]. Investigations on blends of poly(p-phenyl-
ene vinylene) (PPV) with PEO/salt (in a 1:1 ratio)
are summarized in [8]. LiCF3SO3 was added to give
a molar ratio of Li to the CH2CH2O moieties of
1:20. LECs were prepared as thin films of the blend
between ITO and Al. The polymer layer was
prepared by spin-coating a solution of the PPV
precursor polymer and PEO/salt in acetonitrile. A
small amount of water was added to enhance co-
dissolution. The films were then heated to convert
the PPV-precursor into the insoluble and lumines-
Polymer layers were typically 400nm thick. It
was shown that the blends are phase-separated
composites. The morphology of the films depended
on the water content of the solution used for spin-
coating For low and high water contents macro-
100nm occurred. The optimized water:acetonitrile
ratio was found to be 1:5 (by volume). However,
the question of whether the PEO and PPV phases
form an interpenetrating network under these
optimized conditions such that ions can diffuse
through the film without leaving the PEO phase
remains. The paper reports an ionic conductivity of
the layers of 10ÿ7 S/cm, which is one to two orders
in magnitude less than the typical conductivities of
pure PEO films with Li salt at room temperature
The current±voltage curves of these devices
threshold voltages of 2.5V (ITO ) and ÿ2.6V
(ITO ÿ). The threshold voltage increased slightly
with increasing polymer film thickness. After
switching the voltage from 0 to 4V, light emis-
sion was observed in less than 0.1sec and both the
cell current and the light intensity continued to
increase for about 10sec. At equilibrium the exter-
nal quantum efficiency ex (EL) was 0.3 ± 0.4% at
4V. The stress lifetimes depended strongly on the
external bias. At 4V the light emission decreased
The structure of the junction in LEC surface cells
has been imaged by Dick et al [9]. Samples consisted
of a PPV/PEO/LiCF3SO3 film with molar ratios
1:1:0.05 across a 20mm wide gap between two gold
strips. At 4V, light emission was seen as a bright
line just off-center between the electrodes. The
width of the emission zone was approx. 2mm. The
spatial distribution of the potential f between the
electrodes could be imaged by utilizing the optical
beam induced current (OBIC) method: a light beam
is focused on the polymer film and the photo-
current is recorded while scanning the beam across
the electrode gap. In the first approximation the
photocurrent will be proportional to the local field.
These scans revealed a narrow peak in the photo-
voltage located close to the middle of the gap. This
agrees with the predicted potential drop across the
measured in the contact region even though an
ionic double layer must be present at the polymer/
metal interface. Here, the input light was probably
blocked by the opaque electrodes (the light was
incident from the substrate side). The results prove
that a considerable space charge field is established
across the junction for an external bias larger than
the threshold voltage. However, the observed
Polym. Adv. Technol. 9, 461±475 (1998)
junction width is much larger than the typical layer
thickness in LEC sandwich cells. An open question
is whether the model published by Smith can also
be applied to cells with layer thicknesses in the
range of a few hundred nanometers.
deMello et al. have investigated ITO/MEH-
PPV  PEO  Li triflate/A1 devices with a molar
1017 cmÿ3 [13]. According to eq.(16), an equili-
brium ion concentration of 2  1017 cm3 should be
sufficient to decrease the thickness of the diffusive
layer down to 1nm. However, these devices
showed enhanced charge injection and El efficien-
cies (>0.2%) only in the forward direction, but were
rectifying under reverse bias. This was explained
by the low total amount of ions available in the
films. As pointed out above, the ions need to
establish a space charge field at the interfaces
strong enough to compensate an applied voltage
corresponding to the polymer band gap. In their
estimate the device is treated as two interfacial
capacitors separated by the bulk. If the double
layers have a width Dx and the total thickness of the
device is d, the minimum density of mobile ions
needed to establish a potential drop equivalent to
the external bias Vappl. is [13]:
With d = 100nm, Vappl. = 2.2V, e = 3 and Dx =
1nm this gives  = 1.8  1018 cmÿ3 which is larger
than the salt concentration of the investigated
This model also allows for a crude simulation of
the response time t of the devices. For a resistor and
a capacitor in series, t is given by:
Here, the resistor is formed by the ion-conduct-
ing film of thickness d and  is the ion conductivity.
For typical values:  = 10ÿ7S/cm, e = 3, d = 100nm
and Dx = 1nm response times should be in the sub-
millisecond range. However, experiments reveal
much longer time constants. This indicates that the
onset process is far more complicated, including
the diffusion of the ions to the interface, establish-
ing the quasi-equilibrium concentration of charges
in the film and formation of the junction by the
diffusion of the charge carriers into the bulk. Since
the internal field in the polymer layer before
formation of the junction is low, the latter process
might limit the response time of the device. The
situation gains further complexity by the fact that
the negatively charged counterions usually have
much smaller mobilities. Therefore, the formation
of the ionic diffusive layer at the cathode advances
the build-up of the ion layer at the anode. On the
other hand the barrier for charge injection is
commonly larger for electrons than for holes and
the onset voltages for electron and hole injection are
different (see also [13]). For example, Pei et al. have
interpreted the slow onset in terms of the low
mobility of the counterion, but do not give numbers
for the ion conductivity because of the motion of
Fast response times in the range of 10±50msec
have been observed in bicontinuous networks of
MEH-PPV and PEO [12]. The miscibility of the two
polymeric components has been enhanced by
adding liquid surfactants such as octylcyanoacetate
(OCA) to the polymer blend. Bicontinuous phase-
formed with a nm length scale of the domains.
LECs based on these multicomponent layers also
had much higher brightnesses of up to 1000cd/cm2
at 3.0V and external quantum efficiencies in the
range 1±2.5%. The stress lifetime was increased to
about 100hr. This improvement in the response
time might be related to an increase in ion
conductivity or due to enhanced electron or hole
mobility. Unfortunately, no further information
was provided on these properties.
Considering the problems related to phase separa-
tion as described above, it would be desirable to
fabricate single component LECs, which perform
charge transport and ion transport at the same time
and show reasonable luminescence efficiencies.
Recently, Lauter et al. introduced solid polymer
electrolytes based on poly(p-phenylene)s (PPP)
substituted with short oligo-ethyleneoxide side-
chains [18, 19]. The chemical structure is shown in
Scheme 2. Statistical copolymers have been pro-
duced by the copolymerization of monomers with
different side-chain lengths. X-ray diffraction ex-
periments on cast films revealed a layered struc-
ture, with layers of main chains separated by
amorphous PEO side-chain regions. Films have
been prepared on flat substrates either by spin-
coating or by casting from solution. 2D X-ray
experiments on cast films revealed an orientation
of the layers parallel to the film surface with the
side chains extending normal to the layer plane
(Fig. 5). Figure 6 shows the bulk ion conductivities
 of cast films of PPP(EO)x/y as a function of
temperature for different substitution patterns.

LECs were prepared by spin-coating the poly-

shows a gradual increase in current, which can be

EL external efficiency ex (EL), defined as the

LEC device is characterized by a steep increase in

Addition of salt clearly increases the current
density through the device but the EL efficiency has
even decreased in this case. Since ex (EL) can be
expressed in terms of a charge balance factor f(El)
and the quantum efficiency of photoluminescence
(PL), the influence of salt addition on the photo-
luminescence behavior needs to be considered. The
experimental findings are summarized in Table 1.
Adding salt decreases the PL efficiency from about
18% to values between 5 and 12%. Even though this
change in (PL) is quite significant and might in
part explain the low efficiency of these LEC
devices, the data indicate that there is still a
pronounced charge injection imbalance. Obviously
the ionic space charge layers are not fully devel-
oped. Further evidence for incomplete formation
comes from the observation that the threshold
voltage is clearly above the corresponding band-
gap value and that the EL intensity with ITO wired
as the cathode is low. On the other hand, the salt
concentration is comparable to the molar ratios
used by Pei et al. in MEH-PPV based devices. For
PPP(EO)5/6 a molar fraction of 4% per EO unit
corresponds to a molar concentration of 1mol/l
equivalent to more than 1020 Li/cm3. We still have
no conclusive explainations for these findings, but
the particular electronic structure of PPP should be
pointed out. The work-functions of ITO and Al are
4.9  0.1eV and 4.2  0.15eV, respectively [5]. The
cyclovoltammogram of PPP(EO)4/5 in tetra hydro-
furan (THF) and CH2Cl2 solution showed the onset
of an irreversible oxidation above 1V versus Ag/
AgCl and an irreversible reduction at approx.
ÿ1.9eV. Similar numbers have been reported for
alkoxy-substituted PPP [20]. It has been shown that
electrochemical potentials E can be translated into
absolute energies ' by [21, 22]:
'  E  EAg=AgCl  4:6  0:1V
The potential of the EAg/AgCl versus standard
electrochemical potential is 0.22eV. Using these
numbers we obtain a HOMO position of 5.8eV and
a LUMO energy of 2.9eV. The estimation of
absolute numbers from electrochemical experi-
ments is however quite controversial. For example
CV experiments on alkoxy substituted PPV shows
the oxidation at 0.7V (versus Ag/AgCl) corre-
sponding to a HOMO position of 5.5eV [20]. In
contrast VEH calculations give an ionization energy
for the same compound of 4.72eV which agrees
quite well with the value of 4.9eV given by Parker
[5] for MEH-PPV. One general problem of CV-
experiments on most conjugated polymers is their
low solubility in aqueous electrolytes. Therefore
most experiments are done on solutions of the
polymers in organic solvents such as THF or
dichloromethane with a low amount of added salt.
In this case the potential in the solution might be ill-
defined and absolute numbers should be treated
Nevertheless, these estimates predict a large

Finally, we comment on the transient behavior
of a PPP(EO)5/6/LiOTF LEC (Fig. 8). After switch-
ing the external bias from 0 to 6V the EL intensity
increases gradually and saturates after approx.
200sec. Afterwards, it decreases slowly with time.
If the bias is then changed to 5V (slightly above the
onset of the device) the EL intensity decreases
immediately to a lower level. After switching back
to 6V, the EL intensity jumps to a value of half the
saturation intensity, followed by a gradual in-
crease. The ratio of step height and saturation
intensity is a function of the voltage levels and the
time elapsed between voltage steps. Interestingly,
running the device at 2V for only 40sec will almost
reset the device to the original 0V state. The
thermal equilibrium of the off-state is obviously
established faster than the quasi-equilibrium of the
on-state. This supports the idea that an under-
standing of the transient behavior of LECs cannot
be solely based on the consideration of ion motion
but needs to take into account the interaction of the
injected charges and the ionic space charge field.
Recently, Yang and Pei have demonstrated an
poly(9,9-bis(3,6-dioxaheptyl)-fluorene-2,7-diyl)
(BDOH-PF) [24]. The chemical structure of the
compound is shown in Scheme 3. As in the solid
electrolyte material discussed above, ion-conduct-
ing PEO-oligomers have been attached as side-
chains to a conjugated luminescent backbone.
Single layer, single component LEC devices con-
sisted of a 150nm thick layer of BDOH-PF between
ITO and Al. Lithium triflate was added to the
material with a molar ratio of 5.6:1 (BDOH-PF:Li).
The molar Li concentration is thus considerably
higher than that used in most PPV-based LEC
devices. BDOH-PF-based LECs had the current and
light onset at 3.1V. The external quantum efficiency
was 4% and the maximum brightness was 190cd/
m2. These devices are among the most efficient
blue-emitting devices ever reported. It has not been
discussed whether the device characteristics were
(anti-) symmetric about the origin. The EL emission

In the second part of this section, the properties
of LEDs prepared through the layer-by-layer self-
assembly of polyelectrolytes are discussed. This
method is based on the alternative adsorption of
monolayers of polycations and polyanions by
corresponding polyelectrolyte solutions [29, 30].
Hong et al. reported on the characterization of
LEDs from poly(styrene-4-sulfonate) (SPS) and a
copolymer of PPV and naphthalene [31]. LEDs
were prepared by alternative deposition of the
PPV-precursor polymer as the polycation and SPS
as the polyanion. The assembled precursor was
converted to the conjugated polymer by heating in
vacuum. Owing to the high stability of the self-
assembled layers, devices with a layer thickness of
only 20 ± 30nm between ITO and A1 demonstrated
stable emission and a threshold voltage of approx.
2.3V for ITO biased as the anode. In contrast to
most devices discussed above, no emission could
be detected under reverse bias. Even though ions
are initially present they do not severely affect the
electrical properties of the devices. The EL spectra
of the self-assembled devices were quite similar to
those obtained from spin-coated devices (layer
thickness 100nm). This approach thus allows
the preparation of LEDs with very thin emission
layers. The brightness of the devices was, however,
rather low, which might be caused by efficient
exciton quenching at the polymer±metal interfaces.
The emission profile of LEDs with aluminum top
electrodes has recently been measured by us [23]. It
could be shown that the emission is quenched
almost completely within a distance of 20nm from
the Al/polymer interface. Choong and coworkers
have demonstrated that the PL of a 20nm thin PPV
layer is strongly affected by a sub-monolayer
deposition of Ca [32, 33] in agreement with our
LEDs from PPV and two different polyanions,
SPS and poly(methacrylic acid) (PMA) have been
extensively studied by Fou et al. [34] The properties
of devices fabricated from PMA/PPV strongly
current±voltage and luminescence±voltage curves
of devices converted at 180°C for 4hr were almost
surprising observation was interpreted in terms of
a Fermi-level pinning by new states because of
byproducts created in the course of the thermal
conversion. Even though the current densities
supported by these films were high (on the order
of 1 A/cm2), the luminance was rather low (1±
2cd/m2). Conversion at 210°C for 11hr gave
devices with pronounced rectification, lower cur-
rent densities and much higher luminance in the
range of 10±20cd/m2. In contrast, I±V and L±V
curves obtained from PPV/SPS devices converted
at the same temperature were almost symmetrical.
In this case the onset voltages for emission were
independent of film thickness. This is a typical
signature of LEC devices and indicates the forma-
tion of space charge layers by ion diffusion. The
authors further demonstrated that devices based on
an ITO/(SPS/PPV)n/(PMA/PPV)m/Al multilayer
structure exhibited higher EL efficiencies and
luminance levels (100±130cd/m2) than devices
made purely from PMA/PPV. As pointed out by
the authors LEDs based on self-assembled layers
now can deliver efficiencies and luminance levels
similar to devices prepared by spin-casting. The
results on various self-assembled layer devices
strongly on the choice of the polyanion. These
devices exhibit electrical properties similar to either
a conventional organic LED, a LEC or even had
new and unconventional characteristics.

Finally, we would like to discuss recent results on
electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) of films
of redox-active luminescent polymers such as
poly(vinyl-9-10-diphenylanthracene) or MEH-PPV
[35, 36]. Devices were fabricated by casting the
active polymer layer on the working electrode. The
sample was then immersed in a liquid electrolyte to
give a conventional three-electrode-type electro-
chemical cell. ECL occurred upon stepping the
potential periodically between the reduction and
oxidation potential of the polymer. Emission was
generated through the following sequence [36]:
In the modified approach published later, a
strong oxidant or reductant was added, which can
Polym. Adv. Technol. 9, 461±475 (1998)
LEDs Based on Solid Electrolytes and Polyelectrolytes / 473
undergo an electron transfer reaction with the
redox-active luminescent species to generate light
[36]. In this case, the ECL efficiency, defined as the
ratio of the integrated emission intensity and the
forward electrolysis, was 0.4%. As noted by Bard,
ECL involves similar phenomena as the emission
from an LEC [37]. Charge carriers are generated by
electron-transfer reaction at the electrodes (this is
true for every organic LED) and the function of the
added ions is two-fold: to establish an appropriate
potential drop and to provide counterions to the
injected charges. In the electrogenerated chemilu-
minescence experiments, however, charge transfer
occurs always at the same electrode. Light was
generated either by stepping the potential, or by
driving redox reactions simultaneously with two
redox species. Further, ions were not mixed
directly into the layer but supplied by the liquid
electrolyte. Also note, that the films studied by Fan
and Richter did not contain a particular ion-
conducting component. Even though the differ-
ences between the two concepts seem to be minor,
rather faint and the stress lifetime was short. This
large difference in performance requires further

The pronounced differences in the properties of
conventional LEDs and luminescent electrochemi-
cal cell devices can be well understood from the
modification of the electric potential across the
polymer layer owing to the presence of mobile
charges. At high ion concentrations thin ionic space
charge layers are established, which allow for an
efficient electron transfer between the electrodes
and the polymer. At sufficiently high external
voltages thermodynamic equilibrium is established
between the electrodes and the polymer layer and
the current density will be controlled by the field
across a junction region between the electron- and
LEC devices have been fabricated either from
polymer blends or from single components such as
luminescent polymers substituted with PEO oligo-
mer chains or even solid polyelectrolytes. Blue
light-emitting single layer devices with air-stable
electrodes and high external quantum efficiencies
in the range of 0.5±2% have been demonstrated.
These outstanding performances can be explained
by a balanced charge injection into the polymer film
through narrow ionic space charge layers. LEDs
have further been fabricated through the layer-by-
layer self-assembly of anionic and cathodic poly-
electrolytes. Many of these devices show high
stability and can deliver luminance levels similar
to conventional LEDs based on spin-coated layers.
Onset times for current and luminescence are in
the order of milliseconds to seconds and have been
related to the ion conductivities in some cases. An
exact treatment of the onset process, however,
would need to take into account the motion of both
anions and cations as well as the interaction
between the ionic space charge layers and the
injected charges (electrons and holes). Reasons for
the short stress lifetimes of LEC devices, which are
currently up to about 100hr, are still unknown. One
might presume that ions do undergo charge
transfer reactions at the interfaces, forming rather
reactive species, but experimental evidence in
support of this suspicion is still missing.
We would finally like to mention that devices
with emission under both forward and reverse DC
bias have frequently been reported by others (see,
e.g., [38±40]). Similar processes as those discussed
in this paper might also be involved here, but the
extensive discussion of the physics of these devices
is beyond the scope of this paper.
